ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for crab meat and fish maw thick soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of crab meat and fish maw thick soup weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) and Fish maw (swim bladder), dried, reconstituted, estimated from component analysis; adapted composite for Chinese thick soup preparation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for crab meat and fish maw thick soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked crab meat (FDC ID 175180) and reconstituted fish maw, combined with HPB Singapore Foodo data for Chinese seafood soups, and weighted to reflect the dish's typical ingredient proportions (crab meat ~25%, fish maw ~10%, broth ~55%, egg ~5%, starch ~5%). Input record values (energy 70 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 2.5 g, carbohydrate 3.75 g, sodium 300 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, vitamin C) are also consistent with USDA component data and are retained.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on cornstarch-thickened broth soups (GI ~35) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crab meat and fish maw thick soup. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous cornstarch-thickened Chinese soups from the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary carbohydrate source is cornstarch (GI ~68 pure), but at the low concentration used in this soup (~3.75 g carbohydrate per 100 g), the effective GI of the dish is substantially reduced by the protein and fat matrix. GL per serving = (35 × 15 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per standard 400 g bowl), contributed by soy sauce and chicken broth, approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl and request reduced soy sauce and low-sodium chicken broth to keep total sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.75 g per 100 g), low sugar (0.5 g per 100 g), high protein (7.5 g per 100 g), and estimated low GI (~35) from cornstarch-thickened broth matrix
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is a low-glycaemic option suitable for people with type 2 diabetes; however, portion size should be monitored as a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 15 g of carbohydrate, and lighter cornstarch thickening should be requested where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 45 mg per 100 g (180 mg per 400 g bowl) from crab meat and egg, combined with moderate sodium (300 mg per 100 g); saturated fat is low at 0.63 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup in moderation, requesting reduced soy sauce and low-sodium broth, and limiting frequency of consumption given the cumulative cholesterol and sodium load per bowl.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.63 g per 100 g), lean seafood proteins with low arachidonic acid content, and sesame oil providing anti-inflammatory unsaturated fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This soup is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its lean seafood protein and low saturated fat; sodium from soy sauce should be minimised by requesting reduced seasoning to avoid worsening tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Meat and Fish Maw Thick Soup is a classic Chinese banquet-style soup widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish features succulent crab meat — typically from blue swimmer or mud crab — combined with reconstituted fish maw (the dried swim bladder of large fish such as croaker or sturgeon), which absorbs the savoury broth and provides a soft, gelatinous, collagen-rich texture. The soup base is a seasoned chicken broth enriched with soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil, thickened to a velvety consistency using cornstarch slurry, and finished with silky egg-drop ribbons. The result is a warming, umami-rich soup with a smooth, slightly sticky mouthfeel and a delicate seafood flavour. Nutritionally, the soup provides 70 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 7.5 g per 100 g from crab meat, fish maw, and egg, and a moderate sodium level of 300 mg per 100 g. Saturated fat is low at 0.63 g per 100 g. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 35 (low), making it a suitable option for blood glucose management. A standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 280 kcal, 30 g protein, and 1200 mg sodium. Clinically, the soup is low-glycaemic and protein-dense, but sodium content warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Fish maw is prized in Chinese culinary tradition for its collagen content and is commonly served at festive occasions, wedding banquets, and family celebrations.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for crab meat and fish maw thick soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked crab meat and reconstituted fish maw, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for Chinese seafood soups. Input record values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from cornstarch-thickened Chinese soups in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Chinese seafood soup is not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~180 mg per 100 g based on crab meat component data but not confirmed, so -1 is used to preserve numeric hygiene. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite dish are not available in approved sources as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are expected in this traditionally prepared seafood soup; consistent with USDA data for cooked crab and fish products. 5. Halal status is marked false as fish maw soups in Singapore restaurants may use non-halal chicken broth or soy sauce; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 7. Nutrient values are adapted composites; a direct laboratory analysis of this dish would improve confidence to high.
